quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Exponential growth"?

options.text: A grid used to predict genotype and phenotype ratios in offspring from a particular cross
dN/dt = rN; unlimited population growth producing J-shaped curves; occurs when resources are abundant and competition is minimal
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis; increases genetic variation
A simple sugar (glucose, fructose, galactose) with the general formula (CH₂O)n; the monomer of carbohydrates

quiz_questions.explanation: "Exponential growth" is defined as: dN/dt = rN; unlimited population growth producing J-shaped curves; occurs when resources are abundant and competition is minimal. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Logistic growth"?

options.text: A series of protein kinases that sequentially phosphorylate and activate each other, amplifying the signal at each step
The rate at which producers store energy as biomass minus their own respiratory losses; NPP = GPP - respiration
dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K; growth that slows as population approaches carrying capacity K, producing S-shaped (sigmoid) curves
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Logistic growth" is defined as: dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K; growth that slows as population approaches carrying capacity K, producing S-shaped (sigmoid) curves. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Carrying capacity (K)"?

options.text: Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap
Regulatory molecules bind to a site other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation and activity; can be activating or inhibiting
The specific region of an enzyme where the substrate binds and the catalytic reaction occurs; shape determines specificity
The maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources

quiz_questions.explanation: "Carrying capacity (K)" is defined as: The maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "r-selected species"?

options.text: A gene-editing system using guide RNA to direct the Cas9 nuclease to cut DNA at a precise location, enabling precise gene knockout, correction, or insertion
Species with high reproductive rates, short lifespans, and little parental care (e.g., bacteria, insects); adapted to unstable environments
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote (e.g., Iᴬ Iᴮ blood type produces both A and B antigens)
Random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I, producing 2ⁿ possible gamete combinations (n = haploid number)

quiz_questions.explanation: "r-selected species" is defined as: Species with high reproductive rates, short lifespans, and little parental care (e.g., bacteria, insects); adapted to unstable environments. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "K-selected species"?

options.text: A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification
Small intracellular molecules (cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP₃) that relay and amplify signals from the receptor to target proteins
The number of individuals contributing genetically to the next generation; usually much smaller than census size due to unequal sex ratios, variance in reproductive success
Species with low reproductive rates, long lifespans, and extensive parental care (e.g., elephants, whales); adapted to stable environments

quiz_questions.explanation: "K-selected species" is defined as: Species with low reproductive rates, long lifespans, and extensive parental care (e.g., elephants, whales); adapted to stable environments. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "dN/dt = rN; unlimited population growth producing J-shaped curves; occurs when resources are abundant and competition is minimal"?

options.text: Phospholipid
Exponential growth
Entropy
Keystone species

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Exponential growth".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K; growth that slows as population approaches carrying capacity K, producing S-shaped (sigmoid) curves"?

options.text: Electron transport chain
Free energy (ΔG)
Logistic growth
Molecular clock

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Logistic growth".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources"?

options.text: Dominant and recessive
Carrying capacity (K)
Codominance
Biodiversity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Carrying capacity (K)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Species with high reproductive rates, short lifespans, and little parental care (e.g., bacteria, insects); adapted to unstable environments"?

options.text: Photorespiration
Cellular respiration (overall)
r-selected species
Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "r-selected species".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Species with low reproductive rates, long lifespans, and extensive parental care (e.g., elephants, whales); adapted to stable environments"?

options.text: Chitin
K-selected species
Leading strand
Acetyl-CoA

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "K-selected species".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Population Ecology is accurate?

options.text: Population Ecology studies how populations grow, interact with their environment, and are regulated.
Enzymes are typically proteins with a specific active site that binds substrates via the induced fit model.
Proteins are polymers of amino acids linked by peptide bonds, with structure and function determined by the sequence (primary structure) and folding (secondary, tertiary, quaternary structures).
Enzyme specificity arises from the precise 3D shape of the active site, which is determined by the amino acid sequence.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Population Ecology?

options.text: Exponential growth (dN/dt = rN) occurs when resources are unlimited, producing J-shaped curves.
Helicase unwinds the double helix, primase synthesizes RNA primers, and DNA polymerase III synthesizes new strands in the 5'→3' direction.
Enzymes are typically proteins with a specific active site that binds substrates via the induced fit model.
When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Population Ecology?

options.text: Mutation and Cancer explains how accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes (gain-of-function to oncogenes) and tumor suppressor genes (loss-of-function) drive the multistep development of cancer.
NADH and FADH₂ donate electrons to protein complexes (I-IV), which pump H⁺ from the matrix to the intermembrane space, creating a proton gradient.
Logistic growth (dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K) incorporates carrying capacity K and produces S-shaped curves.
Enzyme specificity arises from the precise 3D shape of the active site, which is determined by the amino acid sequence.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Population Ecology?

options.text: Three turns of the Calvin cycle fix 3 CO₂ to produce one net G3P molecule, consuming 9 ATP and 6 NADPH.
Populations are characterized by density, age structure, survivorship curves (Type I, II, III), and life history strategies (r-selected vs.
Genetic mapping constructs a linear order of genes along chromosomes based on recombination frequencies.
Repair mechanisms include base excision repair (BER) for damaged bases, nucleotide excision repair (NER) for bulky lesions, and mismatch repair for replication errors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Population Ecology?

options.text: Monosaccharides join via glycosidic bonds through dehydration synthesis to form disaccharides and polysaccharides.
Population Ecology studies how populations grow, interact with their environment, and are regulated.

In eukaryotes, regulation occurs at multiple levels: chromatin remodeling (acetylation, methylation), transcription factors, mRNA processing, mRNA stability, translation regulation, and post-translational modification.

Translation occurs in three stages: initiation (ribosome assembles at start codon AUG), elongation (amino acids added one by one as ribosome moves along mRNA), and termination (release factor binds stop codon).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Population Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Population Ecology?

options.text: Human population growth following a roughly exponential pattern from 1 billion (1800) to 8 billion (2022), with demographic transition slowing growth in developed nations
DNA profiling (forensic fingerprinting) using short tandem repeat (STR) polymorphisms for criminal identification with error rates below 1 in a billion
HPV vaccines (Gardasil) preventing cervical cancer by protecting against viral proteins (E6, E7) that inactivate tumor suppressors p53 and Rb
Ecosystem services valuation estimating that pollination by insects is worth $235-577 billion annually globally, providing economic justification for pollinator conservation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Population Ecology?

options.text: Invasive species (zebra mussels, kudzu) exhibiting exponential growth upon introduction to new environments lacking natural predators or competitors
Xeroderma pigmentosum caused by defective nucleotide excision repair, making patients extremely sensitive to UV radiation-induced DNA damage
Galápagos finch adaptive radiation: a single ancestor species diversifying into 14 species with specialized beak shapes for different food sources within ~1-2 million years
Mass extinctions (e.g., K-Pg, 66 MYA) eliminating 75% of species but opening ecological niches that enabled mammalian diversification and eventual human evolution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Population Ecology applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Insulin signaling pathway: insulin binding to its receptor triggers a kinase cascade leading to glucose transporter (GLUT4) insertion into cell membranes
Wildlife management using logistic growth models to calculate maximum sustainable yield for harvested populations (fisheries, deer management)
Golden Rice engineered with β-carotene biosynthesis genes to combat vitamin A deficiency in developing countries, preventing blindness in millions of children
Darwin's finch beak evolution on Daphne Major Island: drought years selected for larger beaks (to crack harder seeds), directly observed by Peter and Rosemary Grant over 40 years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Population Ecology?

options.text: Human population growth following a roughly exponential pattern from 1 billion (1800) to 8 billion (2022), with demographic transition slowing growth in developed nations
Biofuel production engineering algae with enhanced Calvin cycle efficiency to convert CO₂ into lipids for biodiesel
Prion diseases (mad cow, CJD) where misfolded proteins induce normal proteins to refold incorrectly, demonstrating the critical importance of protein conformation
Capillary action in plant xylem vessels transporting water against gravity from roots to leaves through cohesion-adhesion-tension

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Population Ecology in practice?

options.text: Invasive species (zebra mussels, kudzu) exhibiting exponential growth upon introduction to new environments lacking natural predators or competitors
Artificial selection in agriculture: selective breeding transforming teosinte (small, hard kernels) into modern corn over thousands of years of human-directed selection
Mitochondrial diseases caused by mutations in ETC complex genes, affecting energy-demanding tissues (brain, heart, muscles) and causing conditions like Leigh syndrome
Crop yield optimization through understanding light reaction efficiency and the effects of light intensity, wavelength, and duration on photosynthetic rates

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Population Ecology.
